summary: > 6.6% of the household Carbon Footprint is linked to the furniture, painting and so on that we use inside our houses. When we think about our carbon footprint at home, we usually focus on heating, electricity, or the car. But a less obvious part of it comes from what is actually inside our homes: the furniture, the paint on the walls, the floor under our feet, the cushions and curtains around us. All of these products need to be made, transported, and eventually thrown away, and each of those steps has an environmental cost.

filename: concepts/behavior-change/Talking about Climate Change ACTION GUIDE.md
label: Talking about Climate Change Action Guide
summary: Never doubt that a small group of thoughtful, committed, citizens can change the world. Indeed, it is the only thing that ever has.' - Margaret Mead, The Need for Conversations, Leading by Example, Taking action and being a positive role model are crucial to ensuring our conversations about climate change are more than just words. When we embody the change we want to see, our actions lend weight to our words, making them far more persuasive, More Science is Not the Answer, Knowing Your Audience, Using the Right Words, Changing how we talk about climate change can make a big difference. Using clear, positive language can inspire action and support for policies to address the crisis urgently. Instead of focusing on sacrifice, we should highlight the opportunities for better health and well-being in a world moving beyond fossil fuels. Avoid scientific jargon and misleading terms. For example, 'natural disasters' are often influenced by human actions, making them not entirely natural. Clear communication can help shift perceptions and motivate collective action towards a sustainable future, Susan Joy Hassol shares an illustrative list in her article The Right Words Are Crucial to Solving Climate Change, Ignore the Outliers, Focus your efforts on the majority open to taking action, rather than trying to convert the dismissive few. Campaigns often target extremes, but targeting the middle 50% under the proverbial curve is where our efforts can make the biggest difference. Engaging the willing majority can build the political will and community support needed for meaningful climate action, Communicating Solutions, Highlighting solutions is as important as identifying problems. Focusing only on problems can overwhelm people, making them disengage. Amanda Ripley highlights the need for hope, agency, and dignity in climate coverage in her article in the Washington Post. Most stories miss discussing solutions, leaving audiences feeling powerless. Highlighting positive steps and celebrating small victories can inspire action. It's important to balance fear with actionable hope and not to overemphasize sacrifice, Showcase exciting aspects of climate action, like the benefits of electric vehicles, induction cooking, and vegetarian diets. The dropping prices of renewable energy and projected exponential adoption curves paint an enabling picture of the future. , As critical as climatic tipping points are to the escalating crisis, positive social and economic tipping points to respond to it are underway and within our control. Encourage participation in grassroots efforts and local initiatives to demonstrate the many ways individuals can contribute to climate change mitigation, Guiding Children Through Climate Conversations, Calling out ‘Greenwashing’, Misinformation, Checklist for Conversations, Talking about climate change can lead to real change.
